questions.prompt: Why do trend-following strategies need volatility-adjusted position sizing?

choices.text: Different instruments have different normal ranges of motion — a 2% move in TLT (treasuries) is huge; a 2% move in TSLA is routine. Fixed-dollar sizing concentrates risk in volatile names: 100 shares of TLT and 100 shares of TSLA have wildly different dollar volatilities. Volatility-targeted sizing (using ATR or rolling stdev) calibrates so each position risks the same dollar amount — fewer shares of TSLA, more of TLT. This is the Turtle Traders' contribution; it equalizes the contribution of each position to portfolio risk and lets you diversify across instruments without one volatile name dominating PnL. Standard in modern trend strategies.

questions.explanation: Equalize dollar risk across positions by sizing inversely to volatility (ATR-based).

questions.prompt: Trend strategy backtests at 38% win rate. Worth pursuing?

choices.text: Yes — trend following is asymmetric: low win rate, but the wins are huge multiples of the losses. A profile of 38% wins averaging $300, 62% losses averaging $80 has EV per trade of (0.38 × 300) − (0.62 × 80) = $64.40 — strongly positive. The psychological challenge is real (most trades are small losers), but the math works. The wrong frame is judging trend strategies on win rate; the right frame is EV-per-trade and the win/loss ratio. Discretionary traders who require 'mostly winning' can't run trend; that's part of why the edge persists. Algos are immune to the psychology, so trend is one of the most algorithmically-friendly strategy families.

questions.explanation: Trend is asymmetric — low win rate, big wins. Judge by EV-per-trade, not win rate.
